speaker: interviewee
text: So she was working in a factory, that much I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's so interesting, because she referred to Mengele as the Monster. And you refer to him as the Angel of Death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each of you had your own vivid imagination--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. That's how we called him, Angel of Death, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And both, I mean, the stories are [INAUDIBLE] striking. They're both--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, he was a monster. Only the name what as he--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: August 2, 1994, Los Angeles. We are interviewing Cathy Weiss. Cathy, I just wanted to return for a moment, because you had made a point of mentioning to us your birthday, that you had-- that-- that you had spent your 18th birthday in Auschwitz. And because you made that point, I was wondering if you could recollect, that day, what your feelings were, what you remember from that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing. Nothing. We didn't-- I didn't even know exactly if it's June the 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other thing-- we have an unusual story with you, with this being with the circus animals. I haven't heard anything like that from anyone else. And you mentioned Berta, your-- the dog that you were so close to. And I was wondering why you said you'll never forget her. What were the feelings that you had with-- with those animals, and in particular, with the dog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bertha was always so nice with me. I was sleeping in that circus wagon. And she was sleeping always in front of it. So I felt, first time since I was there, really secure. Because I knew Berta is watching me, taking care of me. And I hugged her. And she licked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a special relationship. I loved her. And she was the first person-- I mean, first being who loved me, who cared about me, how should I say. So you felt some real bond with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes. I loved Berta very much. There were many other dogs, of course. I fed all of them, because that was my job. But Berta was my friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how old she might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was old, probably seven, eight years old. It was another animal-- a horse, Nora-- that was also quite a good relationship. Nora was OK, nothing special. But she was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: You felt very comfortable with the animals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, with her, these-- with these two. I didn't like to be with the bears at all. I didn't have to go into the cage, only to put it in the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like, actually, this German lady was kind to you, since she took you to the hospital.

words.text: she took you to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was OK, yeah. She didn't know that I am Jewish. If she would have known, she couldn't have been kind. Because she would have been afraid. Or, maybe she was anti-Semite. How would I know? We didn't talk about Jews ever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you started to talk about after you married your husband in '46, and then had your son in '47. You were talking about that period in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you share a little more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then after, when my son was about three years old, I went to work also. My husband was working also. And we had a relatively good, normal life. I did bookkeeping. My husband could not finish in engineering, so he was a technician, textile. But he was very, very talented. He was-- he had many innovations. So financially, we were not in such a bad shape, because he got for the innovation, inventions, also besides the salary.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was deported around the same time your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was deported exactly from the same camp at the same time that we were deported, at the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1947, you were living under communism. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you my husband's name was Sigmund. Yes, we called him Sig. What was life under the communists? It was not good. It was bad, because there was no freedom. Of course, we listened to Voice of America with closed windows, so we knew that that is another world, too. Of course we didn't like them, because they took everything besides that what we have to carry-- my houses, my lands, took away my husband's parents' factory, and the houses and everything. You don't like when they take away what you have. But it was not persecution.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after-- after Nazi persecution, were you more open to living under the communists, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course. We knew that our life is not in danger. We knew that as long as we don't make any political expressions or we don't say anything, then we are safe. We just couldn't say anything. It was no freedom. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewee
text: No talk politics. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You emigrated in '65. Since you were not happy from '47 already, what kept you so long in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because we ask for passport already when we got married, a year or two years later. But we always were refused. We're not allowed to. They did not let out people to Western countries. It took us 15 years, always to apply and apply for passport, until we got it, finally, in 1964. And that's when we got out. We went to Rome, to Italy. And that's where we were for almost-- well, almost six months, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you applied for a visa to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We applied to Israel, but we stopped in Italy. We didn't go further. And then we stayed there for six months. We applied for American visa. We went to the American consulate. And we have to wait there six months until we were rechecked and checked that we are OK. Then we came here April, 1965. And we lived in Los Angeles. My husband was successful. He was very talented. And we lived in Los Angeles for a while. Then we lived in Whittier.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you husband do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had first an engineering job, designing. And then he had a factory. And next, Whittier and Santa Fe Springs. And that's why we had moved to Whittier. And when he died, he had heart disease. He died in 1986. Then, in 1987, I moved back to Los Angeles. And since then, I am here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work do you do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I don't do anything. I did bookkeeping also in the United States. Of course, my son and the family, they live in San Fernando Valley.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He finished university here in Los Angeles, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Gabriell.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you the reaction of people that you lived with in Romania, and then also when you came to the United States. Were you able to talk about the Holocaust, about the wartime years, right after the war? Because you remained in Europe. So what was the reaction? Were you able to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, first of all, nobody wanted to believe it. Because it sounded quite unbelievable that in the 20th century, they just-- can just kill like that, without any reason, so many millions of people. So they didn't believe me, in the beginning. But then they did. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they-- did people ask questions? Did they want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, some. Some. Yes, they did. Some, they did. And here, when I mentioned it also-- when you came from, what did you do? I talked about that. Here, it was also sort of unbelievable. But now, we all know that that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your health? How is your health now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not too bad, taking in consideration all these things what happened. Well, I am very, very nervous. I was always very, very nervous since I came back. Because that was another person who came back. It was a spoiled only child, shattered, who arrived to Auschwitz. And it came back a very, very nervous, destroyed young girl. But that was not in style, psychotherapy and things like that. At that time, psychiatrists were not in style in our area at that time. So recuperating, it was not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You used the word 'destroyed.' And yet, you don't come across--

speaker: interviewee
text: In many ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as a destroyed--

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was not the same person. Not the same person, who was optimistic, loving, believing that the world is beautiful and everyone is wonderful-- no. I learned that not everyone is wonderful and the world is not so beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fortunately, there are some wonderful people. And actually, that's why we came here, because we wanted to try out, how is it to be free once? And it's wonderful to be free. Earlier, when I first arrived, I apologized to you for having some allergies and not being able to speak as comfortably as I would otherwise.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned to me that you've gotten asthma recently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had like a bronchitis for a few years, like a chronic bronchitis, which didn't bother me at all. I didn't care about it. And after the earthquake-- not that right away after the earthquake. After the second earthquake, I just couldn't breathe. And then they took me to the hospital, and the diagnosis was that now you have already asthma. So I have asthma, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you actually think it was anxiety?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's what they told me, that it anxiety, what brought it out. And it made asthma from the bronchitis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel during the earthquake, or right after the earthquake? What did you experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. It was a shock. But the worst for me, it was that I was alone. Because I am alone. And I really think that everything is worse when you are alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been through so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was never alone. Only until I escaped, until I was hiding in the hay, until then, I was not alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nightmares about those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any kind of therapy, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As I told you, it was not in style, the therapy. Somehow, I think it was a bond, some kind of bond, among the survivors. We just somehow kept together. And we talked about it. And maybe that was sort of a help. I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you do have a lot of friends who are survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not a lot. A few.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you talk with those people about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son, would you say he's been affected by your experiences, by your husband's experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, my son?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sure he was. I believe that he couldn't have that carefree, good childhood in that atmosphere. Because I couldn't have been ever so real happy like, in many cases, you see real happiness. Sort of, I was content that was already the maximum that I could achieve. So I think he didn't see that real happiness in his life. And-- and on maybe another thing, that he was the only child, actually. And we were only the three of us, my husband, my son, and me. And I was so scared whenever Gabriell was sick, not to lose him. It was too much love, I guess. And that-- that's not good either, for a child. That somehow holds him back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still that way with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But I was, when he was little. Not anymore, no. Not at all, since-- since--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about with them? Are you very protective with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, as I told you, since Gabriell is also an adult, no, it has changed. Only when he was little.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your husband? Was he very protective of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. He gave him more freedom than I did. They understood better. I was somehow too protective. It was no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband share with you his feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course. We were friends, friends for a long, long, long, long time. Actually, I think that was the basis of marriage. First, friendship-- and, of course, love too, at that age. But friendship was very important part of our relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel towards the Germans now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toward the Germans, per se, as population? I don't say anything. Against the neo-Nazis, I am very much concerned. Though the government, supposedly, doesn't let them flourish as they would like to. But the propaganda is quite strong, unfortunately. And that's why I think that we, and everyone, has to talk about it, to write about it, to have a big exposure to things like that, what we went through and what happened, so people would be aware, to stop before anything starts. So I hope it won't start.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, actually, the news media didn't give the information, because it was not allowed to. We didn't know anything. So how could have we stopped it? Did you have anger after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anger after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --happened with all that anger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. Maybe that's why I consider myself still nervous all my life. Probably that's what it became, just that nervous person. That's it. Because, oh, yes, I was very, very angry. I was angry for everyone whom they killed. I was least angry with the one year, what I went through. I was much more angry what they did to my mother, my father, and to all of the others who died there. Because I survived. For me, it was just a terrible, terrible experience. But if my family would have come back and more people would have come back, I think it would have been much less anger. I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that we haven't talked about that you would like to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to share some pictures with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you feel your son was affected by your experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think so. I-- I that's my feeling. He says that not too badly. But he always-- he admits also that he always felt that he's something differently grown up-- grow or raised as the other children in the school, whose parents didn't go through this experience. And I talk to people his age, like second generation, as we call it. And many of them have a feeling that somehow, they were either too much pampered, or they've had sometime too much sadness. So they didn't feel exactly the same way like the other children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he married to someone who's Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He is married to a very nice lady. Her name is Eva. And she is Jewish. She is from Hungary. Her parents came here in 1956 when was the revolution. They were not deported. Because as we all know, in Budapest, most Jews were saved, who were saved, they lived in Budapest. And because of the Swedish pass, what they got, we heard that, of course. And they survived, the whole family. And Eva was born after the war. They were married before the war. She has a brother who was born before the war. They were hiding in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her parents' experiences are a little bit different from yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They were just hiding. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they do have something in common, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Absolutely. And it's-- they have two children. I have a grandson, Michael--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --a granddaughter, Johanna. Johanna has the name of my husband's mother. And Michael-- Michael is just Michael. But my son has my father's name also, because my son is Gabriell Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your son very protective of his children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he tries not to be. And he says he's not. But I'm afraid he is. He says he is not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that perhaps his feelings about being different or not belonging, they have a little bit to do with also being an immigrant?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that has to do with it, too. I am sure. Absolutely. He was 16 when we came to this county. He didn't speak one single word of English at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was hard on him. It was hard on him to leave Satu Mare, because that was his home. Does--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was then, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does he still have a relationship with people there? Does he correspond with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no. No, nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You learned English quite well, quite well--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --considering when you came here. Did you know English? Did you study English there, before you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was a child, I studied a little English, too, but a little. I studied much more French and German. Besides school, also privately at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew some. But that was the so-called British English, so it took me quite a while to get used to the American English. And my accent is awful, of course. That will stay forever. You've mentioned that there were certain things that you feel did affect your son in the way that you raised him or the family that he grew up in. If you could give him any advice on how to do something differently, what would you tell him to do with his children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't spoil the kids so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Do you think-- Do you think that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because maybe that's not good. I don't know. I don't know. I think that that was my big mistake, that I was always so concerned how he's-- how he is, how he studies, how healthy he is. You know? Because there were no grandparents. There was nobody else. I think. And maybe because that was my nature, too. As I told you, I was nervous. My nervous system is not the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides the asthma, you mentioned having a heart condition, too. Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: That you said you  have a heart condition.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. When the heat-- when we talked--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was joking. No. No, no. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am really fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else we didn't talk about that you'd like to mention? Or should we show the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we can show the pictures, if you want to. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my mother, father, and me when I was probably around one or two years old. And I found this picture in the attic after the war. That's the only picture what I found. It was so dirty, so it was redone a little bit, as well as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother before she was married. I got this picture after the war from my aunt, who lived in Romania, in Arad.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the high school graduation picture. Of course, we didn't get it when it was done. Because at the time the picture was ready, we were already in the camps, since we graduated earlier than the rest of the class. But on the picture, we are on it also, all the five Jewish girls who were in our class. And I got it when I came back from the camp, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which are the five? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's my picture on the high school graduation. Lebi, that was my maiden name. [? Catalin, ?] is Catherine, Cathy, official name in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my friend Agnes, Aggie, whom I call, who I went to school all my life, and I-- from kindergarten to graduation. And we knew each other even before we were born. Because our fathers went to the same high school, and they were friends. And the mothers met when they were pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [? From ?] where--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the right one, the last one--

speaker: interviewer
text: For--

speaker: interviewee
text: The right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the last one on the right? OK, rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You should probably say that when you're describing the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was made when we were about 12 years old. We went to the [INAUDIBLE] school. These are all Jewish girls. Starting from the left, her name is Eva. She lives in Europe. Next to Eva is Agnes. She lives in Los Angeles. In the middle is Ann. She lives in Israel. Next is another Agnes. She was killed in Auschwitz. And the last one to the right is me, Cathy.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Sigmund, whom we called Sig, and me. We were in Washington, DC, in 1984.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Sig, in 1985, our last trip together, when we were in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here we are together, I think, in 1984 or so, where we lived in our home in Whittier.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband's mother, Johanna Robart Weiss, who was killed in Auschwitz in 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was beautiful. I remember her. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's my husband's sister, Olga Weiss, who was killed in Auschwitz in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Gabriell, and daughter-in-law, Eva. They live in the San Fernando Valley.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. This is my granddaughter, Johanna, who got her name after my husband's mother, who was Johanna also.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson, Michael.
